contents.name: Trustor
contents.description: Agent that trusts a given entity. The trustor is necessarily an “intentional entity”, that is, a cognitive agent, an agent endowed with goals and beliefs.

contents.name: Trustee
contents.description: Entity that is trusted by an agent (trustor). It is an entity capable of impacting one’s intentions by the outcome of its behavior.

contents.name: Complex Intention
contents.description: Aggregations of at least two disjoint intentions.

contents.name: Atomic Intention
contents.description: Intentions that have no proper parts.

contents.name: Moment Belief
contents.description: Belief about a moment (of the trustee).

contents.name: Disposition Belief
contents.description: Belief about a disposition (of the trustee).

contents.name: Capability Belief
contents.description: Belief about a capability (of the trustee). The trustor believes that the trustee is able to perform a desired action or exhibit an expected behavior.

contents.name: Vulnerability Belief
contents.description: Belief about a vulnerability (of the trustee). The trustor believes that the trustee can shield its vulnerabilities.

contents.name: Intention Belief
contents.description: Belief about an intention (of the trustee). The Trustor believes that the Trustee intends to perform a desired action or exhibit an expected behavior.

contents.name: Social Commitment Belief
contents.description: Belief about a social commitment (of the trustee). Social commitment is a commitment of an agent A towards another agent B. As an externally dependent moment, a social commitment inheres in A and is externally dependent on B. The social commitments necessarily cause the creation of an internal commitment in A. Also, associated to this internal commitment, a social claim of B towards A is created. Commitments and claims always form a pair that are about a unique propositional content.

contents.name: Social Trust
contents.description: A specialization of ground trust in which the trustee is an agent. The intention belief is specific to this type of trust, as in this case the trustee is a cognitive agent endowed with goals. Intention belief is a belief about an intention of the trustee. The trustor believes that the trustee intends to perform a desired action or exhibit an expected behavior.

contents.name: Weak Trust
contents.description: A specialization of social trust in which the trustor has the belief that the trustee has a commitment to goal c, which the trust relation is about.

contents.name: Trusted Delegation
contents.description: Trusted delegation accounts for trust relations in which the trustor delegates the goal of the trust relation to the trustee. It is a social relator, grounded on Weak Trust, which mediates a delegation relation between the trustor (the delegator) and the trustee (the delegatee).

contents.name: Trustor Commitment
contents.description: A trustor’s promise or firm decision to do something on behalf of the trustee.

contents.name: Trustee Commitment
contents.description: A trustee’s promise or firm decision to do something on behalf of the trustor.

contents.name: Trustor Claim
contents.description: A right of the trustor to have something or get something from the trustee.

contents.name: Trustee Claim
contents.description: A right of the trustee to have something or get something from the trustor.

contents.name: Institution-based Trust
contents.description: In the case of the institution-based trust, the trustee is a social system. Institution-based trust builds upon the existence of shared rules, regularities, conventional practices, etc. and is related to a social system trustee.

contents.name: Social System Trustee
contents.description: Social system playing the role of trustee in a trust relation.

contents.name: Social System
contents.description: Orderly arrangements of social entities that interact with each other, based on established and prevalent social rules that structure social interactions.

contents.name: Trust Degree
contents.description: Trust Degree is a quality that inheres in the trust entity, as trust can be quantified. ONTrust does not commit to any particular scale, which means that the trsute degree can be measured according to a given scale, such as a simple discrete scale like (Low,Medium,High) or a continuous scale (e.g. from 0.0 to 100.0).

contents.name: Belief Intensity
contents.description: How much the trustor is sure of her evaluation about the trustee’s quality, that is, how strongly the trustor believes that the trustee has a certain disposition or not.

contents.name: Performance Level
contents.description: The estimated quantitative level of the trustee’s quality on which the positive expectation is based, that is, how well the trustor believes that the trustee is capable of performing a capability or preventing the manifestation of a vulnerability.

contents.name: Manifestation Likelihood
contents.description: How strongly the trustor believes a disposition of the trustee may be manifested through the occurrence of certain events.

contents.name: Trust Calibration Signal
contents.description: Signal emitted by the trustee either to indicate trustworthy behavior or to communicate uncertainties about the realization of capabilities and the prevention of vulnerabilities. Trust calibration signal (the message) is the propositional content of a trust calibration communication, a communicative act that the trustee (the sender) emits to com- municate either its trustworthiness (in the case of trust-warranting Signals) or uncertainties in the realization of its capabilities, so that trustors can adjust their trust levels (in the case of uncertainty signals). It refers to a disposition of the trustee.

contents.name: Trust-Warranting Signal
contents.description: Signals emitted by the trustee to indicate trustworthy behavior.

contents.name: Uncertainty Signal
contents.description: Signals emitted by the trustee to communicate uncertainties regarding the realization of capabilities and the prevention of vulnerabilities.

contents.name: Trustworthiness Evidence
contents.description: Pieces of evidence that suggest that a trustee should be trusted. Similarly to trust-warranting signals, they suggest that a trustee can realize its capabilities and shield its vulnerabilities. However, differently from signals, which are purposefully emitted to suggest trustworthiness, evidence result from trustees’ trustworthy actions.

contents.name: Influence
contents.description: Factors that can influence (either positively or negatively) the beliefs that compose a trust mental state.

contents.name: Trust Influence
contents.description: Influence whose source is a trust relation.

contents.name: Mental Moment Influence
contents.description: Influence whose source is a mental moment.

contents.name: Trustworthiness Evidence Influence
contents.description: Influence whose source is trustworthiness evidence.

contents.name: Trust Calibration Signal Influence
contents.description: Influence whose source is a trust calibration signal.

contents.name: Trustee Action
contents.description: Action performed by the trustee, which refers to a trust relation.

contents.name: Capability
contents.description: Power to bring about a desired outcome. Capabilities are dispositions usually perceived as beneficial, as they enable the manifestation of events desired by an agent. However, when the manifestation of a capability enables undesired events that threaten agent’s abilities to achieve a goal, it can be seen as a threatening capability.

contents.name: Vulnerability
contents.description: Disposition whose manifestation constitute a loss or can potentially cause a loss from the perspective of an agent.

contents.name: Strong Trust
contents.description: A specialization of weak trust in which the trustor knows that the trustee explicitly commits herself to goal c, to which the trust relation is about, as part of an existing agreement.

contents.name: Trust Calibration Signal Media
contents.description: Communication media, that is, the Instrument used to carry out the communication of a trust calibration signal.

contents.name: Social System Participant
contents.description: Participant of a social system.

contents.name: Social System Participant Trustee
contents.description: Participant of a social system playing the role of trustee in a trust relation.

contents.name: Trust Calibration Communication
contents.description: A communicative act that the trustee (the sender) emits to com- municate a trust calibration signal (the message, the propositional content of this act) to the trustor (the reciver).

contents.name: Trustor Action
contents.description: Trust-based action performed by the trustor, based on a trust relation.

contents.name: Ground Trust
contents.description: A complex mental state of a trustor agent, composed of an intention related to a goal, for the achievement of which he counts upon the trustee, and a set of beliefs about the trustee and its behavior.

contents.name: is belief part of social trust
is belief part of weak trust
capability belief is belief part of trust
depends on social system participant trustee
Substantial
contents.description: In UFO-A, substantials are existentially independent objects, such as the Moon, an enterprise, a person, a horse.

contents.name: Moment Type
contents.description: Type whose instances are moments.

contents.name: Moment
contents.description: Moments (also termed aspects, abstract particulars, or variable tropes) are existentially dependent entities and can only exist by inhering in other entities.

contents.name: Endurant
contents.description: Things or object-like entities. An important characteristic of all endurants is that they exist in time keeping their identity, even if changing in a qualitative way (e.g., a flower’s color which may change from red to brown while keeping its identity).

contents.name: Disposition
contents.description: Dispositions are dependent entities in the sense that depend on other objects to exist. We consider them as properties that are only manifested in particular situations on the occurrence of certain triggering events, and that can also fail to be manifested. When manifested, they are manifested through the occurrence of resulting events and state changes. For example, the disposition of a magnet to attract metallic material.

contents.name: Perdurant
contents.description: Events, occurrences, processes.

contents.name: Agent
contents.description: In UFO-C, an agent is a substantial that creates actions and to which we can ascribe mental states (intentional moments). Agents can be physical (e.g., a person) or social (e.g., an organization). A distinction is made between human agent and artificial agent (both subkinds of physical agent), to differentiate human agents from software (or hardware) agents.

contents.name: Object
contents.description: In UFO-C, an object is a substantial unable to perceive events or to have intentional moments. Objects can also be further categorized into physical (e.g., a book, a car) and social objects (e.g., money, language).

contents.name: Intention
contents.description: Intentions (or internal commitments) are mental moments that express desired states of affairs for which the agent commits to pursuing directly or indirectly by pursuing the coming about of a certain state of affairs (e.g., Mary’s intention of paying a bill using an internet banking system). The propositional content of an intention is a goal. Intentions cause the agent to perform actions, which are intentional events, i.e., events that have the specific purpose of satisfying some intention.

contents.name: Belief
contents.description: Beliefs are mental moments that have a propositional content that agents consider to be true. They can be justified by situations in reality. Examples include my belief that Rome is the Capital of Italy, and the belief that the Moon orbits the Earth.

contents.name: Mental Moment
contents.description: Mental moments refer to the capacity of some properties of certain individuals to refer to potential situations of reality. A Mental Moment is existentially dependent on a particular Agent, being an inseparable part of its mental state. Examples of mental moments include perceptions, beliefs, desires and intentions.

contents.name: Desire
contents.description: Mental moment that expresses the will of an agent towards a possible situation (e.g., a desire that Brazil wins the next World Cup).

contents.name: Perception
contents.description: Mental moment that expresses how agents sense their environment and the things that happen around them.

contents.name: Agreement
contents.description: A social relator that mediates a relation between two or more individuals ?that play different roles in the relation. The agreement is the mereological sum of all social commitments and claims that inhere in the corresponding individuals.

contents.name: Communicative Act
contents.description: A speech act such as inform, ask or promise. Communicative Act is an action.

contents.name: is component part of complex intention
Threatening Situation
contents.description: Situation that may trigger a threat event, which may cause a loss.

contents.name: Loss Event
contents.description: Events that necessarily impact intentions in a negative way.

contents.name: Loss Situation
contents.description: Situations that hurt an agent’s intentions.
